framework.name: Consumer Protections for Artificial Intelligence
framework.issuing_body: Colorado General Assembly; enforced by the Colorado Attorney General
framework.effective_date: May 17, 2024 (with key AI duties effective February 1, 2026)
framework.scope: Colorado SB 24-205 regulates developers and deployers of high-risk artificial intelligence systems that make or substantially factor into consequential decisions affecting Colorado consumers. It requires risk management, impact assessments, disclosures, consumer notice and appeal rights, and reporting to the Attorney General, with separate consumer-facing disclosure duties for AI systems that interact with consumers.
framework.applies_to: Persons doing business in Colorado that develop or deploy high-risk artificial intelligence systems; also any person making available an AI system intended to interact with consumers. High-risk systems are those that make or substantially factor into consequential decisions affecting education, employment, financial services, housing, insurance, health care, legal services, or essential government services. The statute generally targets business entities rather than a specific size threshold, though deployer reasonableness considers size and complexity.
framework.penalties: Exclusive enforcement by the Colorado Attorney General; violations are deceptive/unfair trade practices under Colorado Consumer Protection Act, with no private right of action. Civil penalty exposure is up to $20,000 per violation, with additional penalties possible for violating court orders or injunctions.

control.statement: A developer of a high-risk artificial intelligence system must use reasonable care to protect consumers from known or reasonably foreseeable risks of algorithmic discrimination arising from intended and contracted uses.

A developer must provide deployers with documentation on foreseeable uses, harmful uses, training-data summaries, limitations, intended benefits, evaluation and mitigation, monitoring guidance, and other information needed for deployer compliance.

A developer must publish and maintain a public statement summarizing the high-risk AI systems it develops or substantially modifies and how it manages discrimination risks, updating it after substantial modification.

A developer must notify the Attorney General and known deployers or developers of known or reasonably foreseeable algorithmic discrimination risks within 90 days of discovery or receipt of a credible report.

A deployer must implement a reasonable risk management policy and program for each high-risk AI system, considering recognized frameworks, organizational size and complexity, system scope, and sensitivity and volume of data.

A deployer or contracted third party must complete and retain an impact assessment at least annually and after substantial modification, and review deployments to ensure they are not causing algorithmic discrimination.

Before a consequential decision, a deployer must notify the consumer that AI was used, explain the decision context, provide contact information and plain-language notice, and supply opt-out information where applicable.

For adverse consequential decisions, a deployer must provide principal reasons, an opportunity to correct incorrect personal data, and an appeal process with human review if technically feasible unless not in the consumer’s best interest.

A deployer must maintain a public website statement describing deployed high-risk AI systems, risk management, and the nature, source, and extent of information collected and used, and update it periodically.

A person doing business in Colorado that deploys or makes available an AI system intended to interact with consumers must disclose that the consumer is interacting with AI unless that is obvious to a reasonable person.
